paper_title: Tackling Discrepancies in Trade Data: The Harvard Growth Lab International Trade Datasets
research_answer: Vulnerability to reclassification distortions varies enormously by sector. Electronics is the most exposed: revised product codes (merged, split, or redefined codes) in the electronics chapter represent 7% of world trade — more than half of the sector's roughly 12% share of global trade. The standard 1:1 concordance method also causes the largest sectoral reallocation in electronics, shifting its measured share of world trade from 13.4% down to 12.2% after concordance to HS1992, with trade reassigned to sectors like machinery. At the other extreme, stones and minerals is among the most stable sectors: only 0.2% of world trade within that sector is affected by concordance revisions, out of a total sectoral share of roughly 3% of world trade. This contrast highlights how sectors with rapidly evolving product definitions — like technology goods — face much higher risk of measurement distortion when working with harmonized historical trade data.
supporting_quotes: the electronics chapter, which accounts for approximately 12% of world trade in 2007, includes revised codes—defined as codes that were merged, split, or entirely redefined—that represent a value equivalent to 7% of world trade.

Notably, the electronics chapter undergoes the most extensive reallocation: under HS2007, it accounts for 13.4% of world trade in the year 2007, but this share falls to 12.2% after concordance to HS1992.

At the other end of the spectrum, the stones and minerals chapter is minimally affected by reclassification: only 0.2% of world trade within this sector is affected by concordance revisions, of a total sectoral share of 3% of world trade.
